q: Sound is described as a sequence of what type of waves?
Through what type of media does sound propagate?
During propagation, sound waves can undergo which of the following processes?
Which property of media determines the speed of sound within the medium, affected by temperature?
a: A relationship between density and pressure

q: The rate at which sound is attenuated is determined by which property of the medium?
a: The specific gravity of the medium
The dielectric constant of the medium
The magnetic permeability of the medium

q: For many media, such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is considered what?
Besides density and temperature, what other property affects the speed of a sound wave?
How does the speed of sound vary across different media?
a: It will be different for different media
It will be the same for all media
It increases with the density of the media
It decreases with the elasticity of the media

q: In the formula for the speed of sound in a solid medium, $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$, what does $Y$ represent?
In the formula for the speed of sound in a solid medium, $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$, what does $\rho$ represent?
In the formula for the velocity of sound in liquids, $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$, what does $B$ represent?
In the formula for the velocity of sound in liquids, $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$, what does $\rho$ represent?
In the formula for the velocity of sound in gases, $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}}$, what does $\gamma$ represent?
a: the specific heat ratio of gas

q: In the formula for the velocity of sound in gases, $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}}$, what does $p$ represent?
In the formula for the velocity of sound in gases, $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}}$, what does $\rho$ represent?
How do longitudinal sound waves travel in solids compared to liquids and gases?
a: faster in solids than in liquids than in gases
slower in solids than in liquids than in gases
at the same speed in all three media
faster in liquids than in solids

q: Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are described as what type of properties of the medium?
What are the SI units of Young's modulus?
What are the SI units of the bulk modulus?
The speed of sound within a medium is determined by a relationship between density and pressure, which is also affected by what factor?
Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through media such as air or what other substance?
When sound waves propagate, they can be attenuated by the medium. What does attenuation mean in this context?
Which of the following is considered an elasticity property of the medium?
What type of sound waves travel faster in solids than they do in liquids and gases?
Echoes are produced when a sound wave is reflected from what type of surface?
What is an echo described as in the text?
a: repetition of sound due to the reflection of sound
refraction of sound due to changing medium
attenuation of sound due to viscosity
amplification of sound due to resonance

q: According to the text, when can an echo or reflected sound be heard separately from the original sound?
a: If the source of the sound is much closer to the observer than to the reflecting surface
If the reflecting surface is extremely soft and absorbent
If the sound wave travels through a vacuum
If the source and observer are at the same location

q: Reflection of sound obeys which principles?
What is used to reduce the effect of echo in buildings?
a: walls covered up with padding and floors covered with rugs and carpets
walls made of hard, reflective materials

q: How do materials like padding, rugs, and carpets reduce echo?
a: They absorb the incident sound and reduce the amount of sound reflecting off surfaces
They increase the speed of sound to prevent reflection
They refract the sound waves in a different direction
They amplify the sound to mask the echo

q: Echo can be used to determine what distance?
a: the distance $d$ between a source of sound and the reflecting surface
the length of a sound wave

q: In the formula $v = \frac{2d}{t}$ used for echo, what does $v$ represent?
In the formula $v = \frac{2d}{t}$ used for echo, what does $t$ represent?
a: the travel time to the surface
the period of the sound wave

q: Which animals are known to use echolocation to navigate and locate their prey?
What is echolocation defined as?
a: the special ability of emitting sounds and interpreting the echoes
the ability to detect light using echoes

q: What is a reverberation defined as?
a: a reflection of light or sound waves, or a wide reaching impact of an action
a focused beam of sound energy
the change in pitch of a sound wave

q: What is given as an example of a reverberation?
a: the sound bouncing around in a large speaker
a sound wave passing through a wall
a sound wave reflecting off a mirror
a sound wave creating an echo in a canyon

q: What type of surface is required for echoes to be produced?
The reduction of echo in buildings is achieved by using materials that perform what action on sound?
What is the primary phenomenon behind an echo?
Which of the following is a practical application of echoes?
a: determining the distance to a reflecting surface
measuring the temperature of the air
calculating the density of a liquid
observing the color of a sound wave

q: What does the term 'audible' mean?
What does 'audibility' refer to?
a: the ability to be heard or capability of detecting sounds in certain range of frequencies
the intensity of a sound wave
the speed at which sound travels
the quality of a musical note

q: The human ear is capable of detecting sounds only in what type of range?
What is the lower limit of audibility for humans with normal hearing?
What is the approximate upper limit of audibility for humans with normal hearing?
The lower and upper limits of frequencies a person can hear are known as what?
How can the audible frequency range be determined using a specific device?
a: By using a sound level meter

q: Low frequency sounds which are under the lower limit of audibility (20 Hz) are called what?
Can infrasound be heard by humans?
a: Yes, if the intensity is high enough

q: High frequency sounds which are above the upper limit of audibility (20 000 Hz) are called what?
Can ultrasound be heard by humans?
a: Yes, if the intensity is high enough

q: Which animals use ultrasound in echolocation?
What type of sound do most sonar technologies use?
A dog whistle produces sound at a frequency that is what, relative to human hearing and dog hearing?
a: Above the upper limit of audibility of humans but within that of dogs
Below the lower limit of audibility of humans and dogs
Within the audible range for both humans and dogs
Above the upper limit of audibility for both humans and dogs

q: In quality control, ultrasound is used to check concrete for what?
Besides concrete, what other materials can ultrasound inspect for quality control?
What kind of sound waves does a prenatal ultrasound test use?
a: high-frequency sound waves, inaudible to the human ear
low-frequency sound waves, inaudible to the human ear
audible sound waves, which can be heard by humans

q: In a prenatal ultrasound test, what device transmits sound waves through the abdomen?
What is the purpose of ultrasonic scalers in dentistry?
a: to remove calculus (plaque) deposits from the teeth

q: How do ultrasonic scalers remove calculus?
a: They work mechanically using high frequency ultrasound that effectively blasts calculus
They use chemical solvents to dissolve calculus
They use a high-pressure water jet to wash away calculus
They use laser technology to vaporize calculus

q: During ultrasonic cleaning, what may result from the transmission of high energy ultrasound?
a: the reflection of all sound waves

q: In ultrasonic cleaning, where are cavitation bubbles created?
a: on the surface of the transducer

q: What does Radar stand for?
a: rapid assessment and detection of anomalies
remote access data acquisition and retrieval

q: Sonar is a type of technology that works based on what principle?
Sonar is used by ships for what purpose?
a: navigation at sea and to detect the position of other vessels

q: What can be found using sonar by sending out a signal from a transducer and noting the time interval for the reflected signal?
a: the depth of the sea or the position of the shoals of fish
the type of marine life present

q: Elephants communicate over long distances using what type of sound?
What are the fundamental frequencies used by elephants for communication?
Why are infrasound waves (f < 20 Hz) able to travel over long distances?
a: They travel at a faster speed
They are not affected by temperature

q: Rhinoceroses can communicate over long distances because their lowest frequency is 10 Hz, which results in what?
a: higher speed of sound and greater pitch
lower speed of sound and lower pitch

q: What does a prenatal ultrasound test specifically look at inside the abdomen?
a: the blood vessels of the mother
the skin layers of the abdomen

q: The echoes recorded from prenatal ultrasound are transformed into what?

q: What kind of surfaces is ultrasonic cleaning especially effective in cleaning?
a: irregular surfaces or internal cavities and passageways
smooth, flat surfaces with no obstructions

q: What happens to contaminants in ultrasonic cleaning due to cavitation bubbles?
a: They may be displaced from surfaces
They are solidified onto the surface

q: What is the Doppler effect or Doppler shift?
a: the change in frequency of a wave related to an observer due to the relative motion between the source and observer
the change in amplitude of a wave due to reflection
the change in speed of a wave due to changing medium
the change in direction of a wave due to diffraction

q: Who described the phenomenon of the Doppler effect and in what year?
In the reference frame where the wave medium is considered at rest, what type of motion leads to Doppler shift?
a: relative motion between the source and observer
absolute motion of the source only
absolute motion of the observer only

q: What is a common example of Doppler shift mentioned in the text?
a: the change in frequency (pitch) heard when a vehicle sounding a horn approach and recedes from an observer
the change in color of light from a moving car
the change in temperature of a moving object
the change in air pressure due to wind

q: Which of these is one of the three cases encountered in Doppler effect?
a: The observer is stationary and the source is moving towards or away from the observer
Both the observer and source are stationary
The medium is moving and both source and observer are stationary
The wave is stationary and the observer is moving

q: In the Doppler effect, if the source is stationary, what is the motion of the observer?
a: moving towards or away from the source
moving in a circle around the source

q: What happens to the observed frequency ($f_o$) relative to the source frequency ($f_s$) when the source and observer are approaching each other?
What happens to the observed frequency ($f_o$) relative to the source frequency ($f_s$) when the source and observer are receding relative to each other?
In the Doppler equation, $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$, what does $f_o$ represent?
a: speed of sound in the medium

q: In the Doppler equation, $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$, what does $f_s$ represent?
a: speed of sound in the medium

q: In the Doppler equation, $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$, what does $v$ represent?
a: speed of sound in the medium

q: In the Doppler equation, $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$, what does $v_o$ represent?
a: speed of sound in the medium

q: In the Doppler equation, $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$, what does $v_s$ represent?
a: speed of sound in the medium

q: When is $+ v_o$ used in the Doppler equation?
a: when the observer moves towards the source
when the observer moves away from the source
when the source moves towards the observer
when the source moves away from the observer

q: When is $- v_o$ used in the Doppler equation?
a: when the observer moves away from the source
when the observer moves towards the source
when the source moves towards the observer
when the source moves away from the observer

q: When is $- v_s$ used in the Doppler equation?
a: when the source moves towards the observer
when the source moves away from the observer
when the observer moves towards the source
when the observer moves away from the source

q: When is $+ v_s$ used in the Doppler equation?
a: when the source moves away from the observer
when the source moves towards the observer
when the observer moves towards the source
when the observer moves away from the source

a: the minimum intensity of sound that can be heard
the maximum intensity of sound that can be heard

its intensity, which varies with the frequency

its pitch, which varies with the amplitude

q: What is the intensity of sound at a place defined as?

the rate of sound wave absorption by a surface

a: Loudness is a sensation and hence, unlike intensity, it depends on the observer
Loudness is an objective measure, independent of the observer
Loudness is directly proportional to intensity regardless of observer

less energy is used to produce a larger amplitude
the energy used is independent of amplitude
only frequency affects the energy used

a: The greater the amplitude, the louder the note
